questions.stem: A metric $d$ on a set $X$ must satisfy $d(x, y) = 0$ if and only if which condition?
questions.explanation: The identity of indiscernibles axiom requires $d(x,y) = 0$ exactly when $x = y$. Together with non-negativity, symmetry, and the triangle inequality, this defines a metric.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Which inequality is the triangle inequality for a metric $d$?
questions.explanation: The triangle inequality states $d(x,z) \le d(x,y) + d(y,z)$ for all points $x, y, z$: a direct path is no longer than a detour. It is the key axiom that makes open balls behave well.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: In a metric space $(X, d)$, the open ball $B(x, r)$ is defined as which set?
questions.explanation: The open ball of radius $r$ about $x$ is $B(x,r) = \{ y : d(x,y) < r \}$, with strict inequality. Using $\le$ gives the closed ball, and $= r$ gives the sphere.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The metric topology induced by $d$ on $X$ is the topology generated by which basis?
options: The open balls $B(x, r)$ for all $x \in X$ and $r > 0$.
questions.explanation: Every metric induces a topology whose basis is the collection of open balls $B(x,r)$. A set is open in this topology iff around each of its points there is an open ball contained in the set.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: For functions between metric spaces, the epsilon-delta definition of continuity is which of the following relative to the topological definition?
questions.explanation: In metric spaces the epsilon-delta definition of continuity agrees exactly with the topological definition (preimages of open sets are open). The metric topology is precisely what makes these two notions coincide.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: A sequence $(x_n)$ in a metric space is a Cauchy sequence when which condition holds?
options: It converges to a limit in $X$.
For every $\varepsilon > 0$ there is an $N$ such that $d(x_m, x_n) < \varepsilon$ for all $m, n \ge N$.
questions.explanation: A Cauchy sequence is one whose terms get arbitrarily close to each other: for each $\varepsilon > 0$ there is $N$ with $d(x_m, x_n) < \varepsilon$ whenever $m, n \ge N$. Convergent sequences are Cauchy, but the converse requires completeness.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: A metric space is called complete when which condition holds?
options: Every Cauchy sequence converges to a point of the space.
questions.explanation: Completeness means every Cauchy sequence has a limit within the space. It is the property that makes a metric space free of missing limit points (gaps).
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Is the metric space $\mathbb{R}$ with the usual metric $d(x,y) = |x - y|$ complete?
options: No, some Cauchy sequences fail to converge.
Only the bounded part is complete.
Yes; every Cauchy sequence of reals converges to a real number.
questions.explanation: The real line with its usual metric is complete: this is a basic consequence of the completeness (least upper bound) property of $\mathbb{R}$. Every Cauchy sequence of real numbers converges to a real limit.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Is the metric space $\mathbb{Q}$ of rationals (usual metric) complete?
options: No; a sequence of rationals approximating $\sqrt{2}$ is Cauchy but has no rational limit.
Yes, every metric space is complete.
It is complete only for bounded sequences.
questions.explanation: The rationals are not complete. Rational approximations of $\sqrt{2}$ (such as truncated decimals) form a Cauchy sequence whose limit $\sqrt{2}$ is irrational, so the limit lies outside $\mathbb{Q}$. Density in $\mathbb{R}$ does not give completeness.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Every metric space, as a topological space, satisfies which separation axiom?
questions.explanation: Metric spaces are Hausdorff: distinct points $x \ne y$ at distance $d = d(x,y) > 0$ are separated by the disjoint balls $B(x, d/2)$ and $B(y, d/2)$. In fact metric spaces are normal as well.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The discrete metric on a set $X$ is $d(x, y) = 1$ for $x \ne y$ and $0$ for $x = y$. Which topology does it induce?
questions.explanation: Under the discrete metric, the ball $B(x, 1/2) = \{x\}$ is open, so every singleton is open and every subset is open. Hence the induced topology is the discrete topology.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Two metrics $d_1$ and $d_2$ on $X$ are called (topologically) equivalent when which holds?
options: One is always larger than the other.
They have the same Cauchy sequences only.
They induce the same topology on $X$.
questions.explanation: Two metrics are topologically equivalent when they generate the same topology, i.e. the same open sets. Different distance functions can be equivalent in this sense (for example the Euclidean, taxicab, and sup metrics on $\mathbb{R}^n$).
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: A topological space $X$ is called metrizable when which condition holds?
options: There exists a metric on $X$ that induces the given topology.
questions.explanation: A space is metrizable if its topology can be realized as the metric topology of some metric. Not every topological space is metrizable; for instance, a space that is not Hausdorff cannot be metrizable.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: In a metric space, the closed ball $\overline{B}(x, r) = \{ y : d(x,y) \le r \}$ has which property?
questions.explanation: The closed ball is a closed set: its complement $\{ y : d(x,y) > r \}$ is open. (In a general metric space the closed ball need not equal the closure of the open ball, and it need not be compact.)
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: In a metric space, compactness is equivalent to which sequential condition?
options: Every sequence has a convergent subsequence (sequential compactness).
questions.explanation: For metric spaces, compactness is equivalent to sequential compactness: every sequence has a subsequence converging to a point of the space. This equivalence is special to metric (more generally, to certain) spaces and can fail in general topological spaces.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: A compact metric space has which of the following properties regarding completeness and boundedness?
options: It is bounded but possibly incomplete.
It is complete but possibly unbounded.
It is neither complete nor bounded in general.
It is complete and bounded (indeed totally bounded).
questions.explanation: A compact metric space is complete and totally bounded (hence bounded). Conversely, a metric space is compact iff it is complete and totally bounded. Compactness is strictly stronger than completeness plus ordinary boundedness.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: In the metric space $\mathbb{R}^n$ with the Euclidean metric, the Heine-Borel theorem identifies the compact sets as which?
options: Exactly the closed and bounded sets.
questions.explanation: In $\mathbb{R}^n$ the Heine-Borel theorem says a set is compact if and only if it is closed and bounded. This is a special feature of $\mathbb{R}^n$; in a general metric space closed and bounded does not imply compact.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Does every convergent sequence in a metric space satisfy the Cauchy condition?
options: No, convergence and Cauchy are unrelated.
Yes; convergent sequences are always Cauchy.
questions.explanation: Every convergent sequence is Cauchy: if $x_n \to x$, then for large $m, n$ both terms are within $\varepsilon/2$ of $x$, so $d(x_m, x_n) < \varepsilon$ by the triangle inequality. The converse (Cauchy implies convergent) is what completeness adds.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: A subspace $A$ of a complete metric space $X$ is itself complete (in the restricted metric) if and only if which holds?
questions.explanation: A subset of a complete metric space is complete exactly when it is closed. A Cauchy sequence in $A$ converges in $X$; its limit lies in $A$ for all such sequences iff $A$ contains its limit points, i.e. $A$ is closed.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: On $\mathbb{R}^2$, the Euclidean metric $d_2$, the taxicab metric $d_1$, and the sup metric $d_\infty$ are related how, topologically?
options: They induce topologies that are pairwise incomparable.
They all induce the same (standard) topology on $\mathbb{R}^2$.
questions.explanation: On $\mathbb{R}^n$ the metrics $d_1, d_2, d_\infty$ are all topologically equivalent: each is bounded by a constant multiple of another, so their open balls generate the same open sets. They all give the standard topology.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Is the closed interval $[0, 1]$ with the usual metric a complete metric space?
options: Yes; it is closed in the complete space $\mathbb{R}$, hence complete.
No, Cauchy sequences can escape it.
Only the rational points form a complete subspace.
questions.explanation: $[0,1]$ is a closed subset of the complete space $\mathbb{R}$, and closed subsets of complete metric spaces are complete. Equivalently, $[0,1]$ is compact, and compact metric spaces are complete.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Which of the following functions is NOT a metric on $\mathbb{R}$?
questions.explanation: The function $(x-y)^2$ fails the triangle inequality: with $x=0, y=1, z=2$ it gives $d(0,2) = 4$ but $d(0,1) + d(1,2) = 1 + 1 = 2$, and $4 \le 2$ is false. The others are genuine metrics ($|x^3 - y^3|$ works since $x \mapsto x^3$ is injective).
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: In the discrete metric space on an infinite set $X$, which statement is correct?
options: It is complete but not compact.
questions.explanation: With the discrete metric every Cauchy sequence is eventually constant (terms must be within distance $< 1$, hence equal), so it converges; the space is complete. But an infinite discrete space is not compact (the singleton cover has no finite subcover). It is Hausdorff and totally disconnected.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: What does it mean for a metric space to be separable?
options: Every two points can be separated by open balls.
It can be split into two clopen pieces.
It contains a countable dense subset.
questions.explanation: A metric space is separable if it has a countable dense subset. For example $\mathbb{R}^n$ is separable because $\mathbb{Q}^n$ is countable and dense. Separability is unrelated to the (unfortunately similarly named) separation axioms.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Classify $\mathbb{R}$, $\mathbb{Q}$, and $[0, 1]$ (all with the usual metric) for completeness. Then count.
questions.solution: The real line $\mathbb{R}$ is complete, and the closed interval $[0,1]$ is complete (it is closed in $\mathbb{R}$), but $\mathbb{Q}$ is not complete because a sequence approaching $\sqrt{2}$ is Cauchy with no rational limit. Among these three spaces, the number that are complete is {{1}}. The space $\mathbb{Q}$ {{2}} complete. The space $[0,1]$, being closed and bounded in $\mathbb{R}$, is also {{3}}.
questions.explanation: $\mathbb{R}$ and $[0,1]$ are complete ($2$ of the three); $\mathbb{Q}$ is not complete. $[0,1]$ is closed and bounded in $\mathbb{R}$, hence compact.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Work with the discrete metric $d(x,y) = 1$ for $x \ne y$ (and $0$ otherwise) on a set $X$. Determine the open ball $B(x, 1/2)$ and the induced topology.
questions.solution: For radius $1/2$, the only point within distance $1/2$ of $x$ is $x$ itself, so the number of points in the ball $B(x, 1/2)$ is {{1}}. Since each singleton is therefore open, every subset is open, and the induced topology is the {{2}} topology. With the radius enlarged to $r = 2$, the ball $B(x, 2)$ equals the whole space, so $B(x, 2)$ {{3}} every point of $X$.
questions.explanation: $B(x,1/2) = \{x\}$ has $1$ point, so all singletons are open and the topology is discrete. Since all distances are at most $1 < 2$, the ball $B(x,2)$ contains every point of $X$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Test whether $d(x, y) = (x - y)^2$ is a metric on $\mathbb{R}$ using the triangle inequality with $x = 0$, $y = 1$, $z = 2$.
questions.solution: Compute the candidate distances: $d(0, 2) = (0 - 2)^2 = 4$, while $d(0, 1) + d(1, 2) = 1 + 1$, which equals {{1}}. The triangle inequality demands $d(0,2) \le d(0,1) + d(1,2)$, but $4 \le 2$ {{2}}. Therefore $d(x,y) = (x-y)^2$ {{3}} a metric on $\mathbb{R}$.
questions.explanation: Here $d(0,1) + d(1,2) = 1 + 1 = 2$, yet $d(0,2) = 4 > 2$, so the triangle inequality fails. Hence $(x-y)^2$ is not a metric.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Use the metric-space facts on separation and compactness. Let $(X, d)$ be a metric space.
questions.solution: Distinct points at positive distance are separated by disjoint balls of half that distance, so every metric space {{1}} Hausdorff. In a metric space, compactness is equivalent to sequential compactness, so a compact metric space has the property that every sequence {{2}} a convergent subsequence. The number of the axioms T1 and T2 that a metric space satisfies is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Metric spaces are Hausdorff (disjoint balls separate points). Compactness equals sequential compactness in metric spaces, so every sequence has a convergent subsequence. A metric space satisfies both T1 and T2, so the count is $2$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology
